feed.title: Wasil Zafar — Life sciences
feed.subtitle: Latest life sciences articles from Wasil Zafar

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 12: Evolutionary Genomics
entry.summary: The ability to read and compare entire genomes has transformed evolutionary biology from a discipline of inference to one of direct observation. Comparative genomics allows us to quantify evolutionary relationships at the nucleotide level, identify functional elements preserved across hundreds of mi

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 11: Paleontology & Fossil Interpretation
entry.summary: The fossil record is evolution's physical archive — preserved remains and traces of organisms that once lived. While incomplete (most organisms decompose without a trace), the fossils we do have provide direct evidence of life's history spanning 3.5 billion years. Fossilization is extraordinarily ra

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 10: Mathematical & Theoretical Evolution
entry.summary: Fitness landscapes are one of the most powerful metaphors in evolutionary biology — a way to visualise how genotypes map to fitness and understand why evolution sometimes gets "stuck" at suboptimal solutions. Sewall Wright (1932) introduced the concept of a fitness landscape as a topographical map w

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 9: Behavioral & Social Evolution
entry.summary: Darwin himself called it "one special difficulty" — why would an organism sacrifice its own reproductive fitness to help another? If natural selection rewards individuals that leave the most offspring, how can behaviours that reduce personal reproduction ever evolve? The answer has reshaped our unde

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 8: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo)
entry.summary: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo) is the field that bridges two great biological disciplines: evolutionary biology (how species change over time) and developmental biology (how a single fertilised egg becomes a complex organism). Its central revelation is breathtaking: animals as differe

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 7: Mass Extinctions & Biodiversity
entry.summary: Life on Earth has endured at least five catastrophic mass extinctions — episodes where more than 75% of species disappeared in a geologically brief interval. These are not gradual fade-outs; they are evolutionary crises that reset the trajectory of life, clearing ecological space for new lineages to

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 6: Co-evolution & Symbiosis
entry.summary: No species exists in isolation. Every organism interacts with others — competitors, predators, prey, symbionts, parasites, and mutualists. These interactions are powerful engines of evolution, driving the diversification, adaptation, and extinction of species across the tree of life. Mutualism is an

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 5: Human Evolution & Migration
entry.summary: The story of human evolution stretches back over 65 million years to the earliest primates. Understanding our lineage requires tracing a complex, branching tree — not a single linear chain — through dozens of species, only one of which (us) survives today. Think of it as a dense bush rather than a l

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 4: Phylogenetics & Taxonomy
entry.summary: Phylogenetics is the science of reconstructing the evolutionary history of organisms — mapping the branching patterns of descent from common ancestors. The result is a phylogenetic tree (or phylogeny), a visual hypothesis of how species are related. "Tree thinking" is the ability to read, interpret,

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 3: Speciation & Adaptive Radiation
entry.summary: "What is a species?" seems like a simple question, yet biologists have debated it for over 200 years. The answer matters enormously — species are the fundamental units we count when measuring biodiversity, the units we protect with conservation laws, and the units whose origins we seek to explain. B

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 2: Genetics of Evolution
entry.summary: Evolution ultimately operates on DNA — the molecule that stores heritable information. While Darwin knew nothing of DNA (it was discovered structurally in 1953 by Watson and Crick, building on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography), understanding molecular biology is now essential for explaining

entry.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 1: Darwin, Wallace & Natural Selection
entry.summary: The idea that species change over time did not spring fully formed from a single mind. Before Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, several thinkers laid crucial groundwork — from ancient Greek philosophers who speculated about change in nature, to 18th-century naturalists who c

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 20: Clinical Biochemistry & Diagnostics
entry.summary: Clinical biochemistry (also called clinical chemistry or chemical pathology) applies the principles of biochemistry to the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of disease . Every blood test you've ever had — glucose, cholesterol, liver enzymes, kidney function — is clinical biochemistry in action.

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 19: Molecular Basis of Disease
entry.summary: Every disease, at its most fundamental level, is a biochemical disturbance — a disrupted enzyme, a misfolded protein, a dysregulated signalling pathway, or an uncontrolled metabolic process. From the single amino acid change in sickle cell disease to the complex metabolic reprogramming of cancer, un

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 18: Tissue-Specific Metabolism Integration
entry.summary: The human body continuously transitions between metabolic states based on nutrient availability , hormonal signals , and energy demands . Understanding these transitions is the culmination of everything we've studied: glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, β-oxidation, ketogenesis, the TCA cycle, oxidative ph

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 17: Adipose Tissue & Whole-Body Energy Balance
entry.summary: Adipose tissue is far more than a passive storage depot for fat — it is a dynamic endocrine organ that regulates energy balance, glucose homeostasis, inflammation, and reproduction through secretion of hormones called adipokines . The human body contains several functionally distinct types of adipos

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 16: Immune System Biochemistry
entry.summary: The immune system is a biochemical defence network that distinguishes self from non-self and eliminates pathogens, damaged cells, and tumour cells. It operates through two complementary arms: innate immunity (rapid, non-specific, no memory) and adaptive immunity (slower, highly specific, immunologic

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 15: Digestive System Biochemistry
entry.summary: The gastrointestinal (GI) tract is a remarkable 9-metre biochemical processing plant that converts complex food into absorbable nutrients. Think of it as an assembly line running in reverse — instead of building products, it systematically disassembles macromolecules into their monomeric building bl

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 14: Endocrine System Biochemistry
entry.summary: Hormones are chemical messengers synthesised by endocrine glands and released into the bloodstream to act on distant target cells. Despite enormous structural diversity, all hormones fall into three chemical classes — peptide/protein , steroid , and amine — and understanding which class a hormone be

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 13: Kidney Biochemistry & Acid–Base Control
entry.summary: The kidneys are remarkable organs — each weighing only ~150 g but receiving 20–25% of cardiac output (~1.2 L/min). They filter ~180 L of plasma daily yet produce only ~1.5 L of urine, meaning 99% of filtered fluid is reabsorbed. Think of the kidney as a precision recycling plant: it first casts a wi

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 12: Liver Biochemistry
entry.summary: The liver is the body's largest internal organ (~1.5 kg) and the metabolic command centre — receiving 25% of cardiac output despite representing only ~2% of body weight. Its unique dual blood supply (hepatic artery + portal vein) gives it first-pass access to nutrients absorbed from the gut and make

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 12: Regional Dissection Mastery
entry.summary: The upper limb is a masterpiece of evolutionary engineering — a structure that has traded weight-bearing stability for extraordinary dexterity and range of motion. From the freely mobile shoulder girdle to the precision grip of the hand, every anatomical detail reflects millions of years of adaptati

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 11: Heart & Muscle Biochemistry
entry.summary: The heart is the most metabolically active organ in the body. Beating roughly 100,000 times per day , it consumes approximately 6 kg of ATP daily — more than 20 times its own weight. Yet the heart stores only enough ATP for about 10 seconds of contraction, making continuous oxidative phosphorylation

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 11: Functional & Applied Anatomy
entry.summary: Biomechanics is where physics meets anatomy — the study of forces acting on and generated by the living body. Every time you lift a coffee cup, climb stairs, or turn your head, your musculoskeletal system is solving a complex engineering problem: how to move bones efficiently through space using mus

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 10: Brain & Nervous System Biochemistry
entry.summary: The human brain accounts for only ~2% of body weight (~1.4 kg) yet consumes ~20% of total body glucose and ~20% of oxygen at rest. Unlike skeletal muscle, which can switch between glucose, fatty acids, and ketone bodies, the brain is an obligate glucose consumer under normal fed conditions. This met

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 10: Embryology & Developmental Anatomy
entry.summary: Human development begins at the moment of fertilization and proceeds through a breathtaking sequence of events — from a single cell to a recognizable human form in just eight weeks. Understanding early development is essential not only for embryologists but for every clinician, since congenital malf

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 9: Nucleic Acids & Gene Expression
entry.summary: Nucleotides are the monomeric building blocks of DNA and RNA, but they also serve as energy carriers (ATP, GTP), signaling molecules (cAMP, cGMP), and coenzyme components (NAD⁺, FAD, CoA). Each nucleotide consists of three parts: a nitrogenous base , a pentose sugar (ribose in RNA, deoxyribose in DN

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 9: Histology & Microscopic Anatomy
entry.summary: Before we can understand tissues, we must understand the cells that compose them. Every cell is a microscopic city — with power plants, factories, highways, and communication systems. The invention of the electron microscope in the 1930s opened a new world: where light microscopy could resolve struc

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 8: Signal Transduction & Cell Communication
entry.summary: Cells in a multicellular organism do not operate in isolation — they constantly communicate with each other through chemical signals . A hormone released by the pancreas instructs liver cells to store glucose; a neurotransmitter released at a synapse triggers a muscle contraction; a growth factor te

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 8: Surface Anatomy & Clinical Imaging
entry.summary: Surface anatomy is the art of reading the body's exterior to understand its interior. Before imaging technology existed, clinicians relied entirely on palpation, percussion, and auscultation to assess internal organs. Even today, surface anatomy remains the foundation of the physical examination — e

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 7: Citric Acid Cycle & Oxidative Phosphorylation
entry.summary: Before pyruvate can enter the citric acid cycle, it must be converted to acetyl-CoA — the universal two-carbon unit that feeds the cycle. This irreversible reaction is catalyzed by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDH) , a massive multi-enzyme assembly located in the mitochondrial matrix . PDH is

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 7: Head, Neck & Special Senses
entry.summary: The head and neck region is the most anatomically dense area of the human body. Within a small volume, it houses the brain, the beginnings of the respiratory and digestive tracts, all special sense organs, and a complex network of vessels and nerves. Understanding this region requires precise knowle

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 6: Metabolism & Bioenergetics
entry.summary: Every living cell requires a continuous supply of energy to maintain its structure, synthesize macromolecules, transport ions, and perform mechanical work. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) serves as the universal energy currency — a remarkably versatile molecule that couples energy-releasing (exergonic)

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 6: Visceral Anatomy — Thorax & Abdomen
entry.summary: The thorax houses the body's vital cardiopulmonary machinery within a protective bony cage formed by the ribs, sternum, and thoracic vertebrae. Understanding thoracic visceral anatomy is essential for interpreting chest X-rays, performing procedures like thoracentesis, and managing emergencies from

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 5: Carbohydrates & Lipids
entry.summary: Carbohydrates are the most abundant organic molecules on Earth, with the general formula (CH₂O) n — literally "hydrates of carbon." Think of them as biological solar panels : plants capture solar energy during photosynthesis and store it in glucose; animals then harvest that energy through cellular

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 5: Nervous System & Neuroanatomy
entry.summary: The central nervous system (CNS) — comprising the brain and spinal cord — is the command centre of the entire body. It receives sensory input, integrates and processes information, and generates motor commands. The brain alone contains roughly 86 billion neurons , each forming up to 10,000 synaptic

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 4: Enzymes & Catalysis
entry.summary: Enzymes are biological catalysts — mostly proteins — that accelerate chemical reactions by factors of 10⁶ to 10¹⁷ without being consumed. They are the molecular machines that make life possible: virtually every metabolic reaction in living cells is catalyzed by a specific enzyme. Without enzymes, re

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 4: Cardiovascular & Lymphatic Anatomy
entry.summary: The heart is arguably the most iconic organ in human anatomy — a hollow, muscular pump roughly the size of a clenched fist, situated in the mediastinum of the thoracic cavity between the lungs. Beating approximately 100,000 times per day, it propels around 7,500 litres of blood through nearly 100,00

entry.title: Physiology Series Part 12: Integrative & Clinical Physiology
entry.summary: The stress response is the body's most dramatic demonstration of integrative physiology — a coordinated, multi-system reaction designed to maintain survival in the face of threat. What began as an evolutionary adaptation to predators now activates in response to examinations, traffic jams, and publi

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 3: Amino Acids & Protein Structure
entry.summary: Amino acids are the monomeric building blocks of proteins — the workhorses of biology. There are 20 standard amino acids encoded by the genetic code, each sharing a common backbone but differing in their side chain (R group), which determines their chemical personality. Understanding amino acid chem

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 3: Muscular System & Movement
entry.summary: The human body contains approximately 700 named skeletal muscles , accounting for roughly 40–45 % of total body mass in males and ~36 % in females. All muscle tissue shares the fundamental property of contractility — the ability to generate force by converting chemical energy (ATP) into mechanical w

entry.title: Physiology Series Part 11: Reproductive & Developmental Physiology
entry.summary: The male reproductive system is optimised for one purpose: the continuous production and delivery of spermatozoa. Unlike the cyclical nature of female reproduction, male reproductive function is continuous from puberty , with the testes producing approximately 200–300 million sperm daily . This rema

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 2: Water, pH & Biological Buffers
entry.summary: Water is arguably the most important molecule in biology. Although deceptively simple — just two hydrogen atoms covalently bonded to one oxygen atom — water's unique physical and chemical properties underpin virtually every biochemical reaction. Life as we know it evolved in an aqueous environment,

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 2: Skeletal System & Joints
entry.summary: Osteology is the branch of anatomy devoted to the study of bones. The adult human skeleton comprises 206 named bones , each a living, dynamic organ capable of growth, repair, and remodelling. Far from being static scaffolding, bone tissue is constantly renewed — roughly 10 % of the adult skeleton is

entry.title: Physiology Series Part 10: Blood & Immune Physiology
entry.summary: Blood is the body's liquid connective tissue — approximately 5 litres in an average adult, constituting ~7-8% of body weight. It performs three broad categories of function: transport (O₂, CO₂, nutrients, hormones, waste), regulation (pH buffering, temperature distribution, osmotic balance), and pro

entry.title: Biochemistry Series Part 1: Biological Chemistry Fundamentals
entry.summary: Every molecule in your body — every enzyme, every strand of DNA, every lipid in your cell membranes — is built from atoms obeying the same fundamental rules as the rest of the universe. Biological chemistry begins by understanding how atoms form bonds, and how the geometry of those bonds determines

entry.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 1: Anatomical Terminology & Body Planes
entry.summary: Before we can study any organ, muscle, or nerve, we need a shared language. Anatomical terminology is the universal vocabulary that allows healthcare professionals worldwide to describe the exact location of structures, the direction of movements, and the planes through which the body is viewed. Wit

entry.title: Physiology Series Part 3: Cardiac Electrophysiology & Hemodynamics
entry.summary: The heart is unique among muscles — it generates its own rhythmic electrical impulses without any external nervous input. Cut every nerve to the heart, and it still beats. This property, called automaticity , arises from specialised pacemaker cells that spontaneously depolarise, setting the tempo fo

entry.title: Physiology Series Part 9: Cellular & Membrane Physiology
entry.summary: Every living cell is defined by its plasma membrane — a dynamic, selectively permeable barrier only 7–8 nm thick that separates the precisely controlled intracellular environment from the chaos of the extracellular world. The membrane is not a passive wall; it is a bustling molecular landscape of li

entry.title: Physiology Series Part 7: Endocrine Regulation & Metabolism
entry.summary: The endocrine system is the body's chemical messaging network — a collection of glands and tissues that secrete hormones into the bloodstream to regulate virtually every physiological process: growth, metabolism, reproduction, stress responses, and homeostasis. While the nervous system transmits rap
